title: IMO approves net-zero regulations for global shipping
claim_supported: IMO's MEPC 83 (7-11 April 2025) approved the Net-Zero Framework: mandatory well-to-wake GHG fuel-intensity limits plus a global pricing/reward mechanism (remedial units for deficits, rewards for zero/near-zero tech) for ships over 5,000 GT (85% of international shipping CO2), described as the first mandatory-plus-pricing regime for an entire global industry sector, with entry into force expected 2027 pending formal adoption (originally scheduled October 2025, adjourned to October 2026).
locator: IMO press briefing page, MEPC 83 outcome
notes: Official IMO page fetched successfully this session. Note: as of this session, formal adoption remained adjourned to October 2026 per subsequent WebSearch results (zerocarbonshipping.com, DNV) -- the IMO page itself frames approval as of April 2025; the adjournment/entry-into-force slippage to 'at the earliest 1 March 2028' is cross-referenced from a separate source and should be reconciled by the verifier.

title: Renewable and low-carbon fuels in maritime transport (FuelEU Maritime Regulation (EU) 2023/1805)
claim_supported: Regulation (EU) 2023/1805 'FuelEU Maritime' (adopted 13 Sept 2023, applied from 1 Jan 2025) requires passenger and container ships to use onshore power supply (OPS) or a zero-emission alternative for all electricity needs while moored at major EU (TEN-T core) ports from 1 January 2030 (extending to all ports from 2035), with penalty revenue required to fund maritime decarbonization projects, alongside a GHG-intensity reduction trajectory (2% in 2025 rising to 80% by 2050).
notes: Official EUR-Lex summary fetched successfully this session; specific penalty rate (EUR1.50/unused kWh, per cross-referenced trade sources) not independently confirmed on this specific fetched page -- flagged for verifier to confirm against the full regulation text or a class-society summary (e.g., DNV, LR).

locator: CARB 'New California requirements for on-road and off-road heavy-duty vehicles' page

title: A Framework to Analyze the Requirements of a Multiport Megawatt-Level Charging Station for Heavy-Duty Electric Vehicles
claim_supported: NREL research (Article No. 3788) develops a framework to analyze requirements for multiport megawatt-level charging stations for heavy-duty electric vehicles, part of NREL's broader Megawatt Charging Emulator research program targeting up to 20 MW site-level charging design and grid-impact evaluation.
notes: WebFetch attempt on the direct PDF host (docs.nrel.gov) failed with a DNS resolution error this session; the research-hub.nrel.gov listing page itself was not independently re-fetched -- details taken from WebSearch synthesis of nrel.gov/news and research-hub listing snippets. Flagged for verifier to fetch directly.

title: Megawatt Charging System (MCS) technology page and IEC TS 63379 standardization
authors_or_org: CharIN e.V. (Charging Interface Initiative); International Electrotechnical Commission (IEC)
claim_supported: The Megawatt Charging System (MCS) standard supports up to 1,250V/3,000A DC (over 3.75 MW peak), with CharIN-member contributions formalized in IEC TS 63379 (published Feb 2026) for global interoperability; targeted at Class 6-8 trucks, buses, ferries/marine vessels, light electric aircraft, and heavy-duty mining equipment.
notes: Official CharIN consortium page fetched successfully this session. Reclassified from 'market_industry' to 'standard' / T1 because IEC TS 63379 is a formally published IEC technical specification (not merely vendor marketing), consistent with SOURCE_STANDARDS' T1 definition covering official standards. Geography (DE) reflects CharIN e.V.'s German registered-association headquarters, not a claim about IEC's or the standard's true multinational origin.

title: FLXdrive Battery-Electric Locomotive (product page)
claim_supported: Wabtec's FLXdrive product line spans Heavy-Haul (7.0 MWh battery / 3.2 MW traction), Switcher (2.7 MWh / 1.7 MW), Shunter (1.2 MWh / 0.7 MW), and a European Dual-Mode variant (1.5-2.0 MWh / 0.8-5.5 MW), using liquid-cooled batteries with a proprietary thermal-management system, regenerative braking, and DC fast-charging via stationary pantograph; marketed as the world's first 100% battery-electric heavy-haul locomotive. No public pricing is disclosed.
notes: Vendor product page fetched successfully this session; no price data was available on the page (a recurring pattern for heavy-electrification hardware, consistent with the L02/L08 lanes' prior findings of opaque vendor pricing).

title: T 264 Battery Electric mining truck (product page)
claim_supported: Liebherr's T264 Battery Electric mining truck offers up to 240t payload with a 3.2 MWh battery (developed with Fortescue Zero), static quick-charge in 12-58 minutes, compatibility with a Trolley Assist overhead-pantograph system and 'Liebherr Power Rail' dynamic charging, and a claimed 86% reduction in energy cost per tonne versus diesel for a full electric fleet. No public price is disclosed.
locator: liebherr.com T264 Battery Electric product page
notes: Not independently WebFetched this session (details from WebSearch synthesis of the Liebherr product page and Equipment World reporting). Geography (DE) reflects Liebherr's German/Swiss group headquarters, not necessarily the truck's manufacturing site.

title: R1700 XE Underground Battery-Electric LHD Loader (product page)
claim_supported: Caterpillar's R1700 XE is a zero-tailpipe-emission battery-electric underground LHD loader that quick-charges in under 20 minutes when paired with two Cat MEC500 chargers. No public price is disclosed.
notes: WebFetch failed with a connection reset error this session; details taken from WebSearch synthesis (cat.com and Foley Equipment dealer listing snippets).

title: Pioneering technology to electrify Japan's mining industry (Hitachi Construction Machinery / ABB full-battery dump truck, Kansanshi mine)
claim_supported: ABB and Hitachi Construction Machinery jointly developed (since 2021) the world's first ultra-large fully electric haul truck, using a hybrid energy system (high-capacity battery + overhead trolley on grade + regenerative braking); the truck was shipped to Zambia's Kansanshi copper-gold mine (First Quantum Minerals) during 2024, and Hitachi and Komatsu (both Japanese OEMs) separately announced a joint team to develop integrated mine-electrification solutions.
locator: new.abb.com press release; cross-referenced with electrek.co and im-mining.com reporting
notes: WebFetch timed out on the primary ABB press release this session; details taken from WebSearch synthesis, cross-referenced across ABB, Hitachi Construction Machinery, Sustainability Magazine, and Electrek reporting for consistency. Geography includes Japan (Hitachi), Switzerland (ABB HQ), and Zambia (deployment site).

title: Singapore Introduces New Standard for Electric Harbour Craft (e-HC) Charging Infrastructure (Technical Reference TR 136)
authors_or_org: Maritime and Port Authority of Singapore (MPA); Enterprise Singapore
claim_supported: MPA and Enterprise Singapore published Technical Reference (TR) 136 (25 March 2025) establishing safety requirements for charging infrastructure and battery-swap systems for electric harbour craft (e-HC), protecting personnel/property from electrical and mechanical hazards and promoting interoperability (aligned with CCS, IEC 61851, IEC 62840); from 2030 all new harbour craft operating in the Port of Singapore must be fully electric, B100-biofuel-capable, or compatible with net-zero fuels.
locator: MPA media centre release, 25 March 2025 (Technical Reference 136)
notes: Official MPA (Singapore government statutory board) page fetched successfully this session; TR136 scope, date, and the 2030 all-electric-harbour-craft requirement confirmed directly from the fetched page.
